DefVerify reveals gaps between the intended definition and model behavior on six popular hate‑speech benchmark datasets.
An efficient cross-modal prompt interaction module enables lightweight early-stage cross-modal information interaction using prompts as a medium.
A domain-general prompt generation module adaptively blends domain-specific news features to generate domain-general prompts, improving domain generalization for fake news detection.
An image semantic enhancement module performs image-to-text translation to fully exploit semantic discriminative information of the image modality.
The proposed CPLSD approach achieves superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art multi-modal fake news detection methods on three benchmarks.
An evaluation of machine-generated text detectors across texts with varying writing styles reveals high sensitivity of classifiers to stylistic changes.
Detection classifiers can degrade to random performance under certain stylistic variations.
Detection systems misclassify easy-to-read texts while maintaining high performance on complex texts.
The reliability of machine-generated text detection systems is compromised by sensitivity to style and reading difficulty.
A 15K multiple-choice question answering dataset for proportional analogy completion is introduced.
Prompts are augmented with exemplar, structured, and targeted knowledge to evaluate large language models on proportional analogies.
Providing targeted knowledge to prompts assists large language models in completing proportional analogies more effectively than providing exemplars or structured knowledge.
Contemporary large language models achieve limited accuracy on proportional analogy tasks, indicating the task remains challenging.
ICMA combines temporal variability with position encoding that has extrapolation properties to encode sequences.
ICMA expands the model’s view of user behavior and captures long-term user dependencies more effectively.
ICMA includes a multi-view data augmentation method that applies random augmentations (crop, mask, reorder) and insertion and substitution operations that utilize item relevance.

ICMA learns intent distributions through clustering and integrates them into the sequential recommendation model via contrastive self-supervised learning that maximizes consistency between sequence views and their corresponding intents.
ICMA’s training process alternates between an Expectation step and a Maximization step.
ICMA achieves higher recommendation performance than most baseline methods on three real datasets.
An LLM-based simulation framework for social opinion networks evaluates and counteracts polarization phenomena.
The framework constructs three typical network structures to simulate distinct characteristics of social interactions.
Within the simulation, agents interact according to recommendation algorithms and update their strategies through reasoning and analysis.
The framework reproduces opinion polarization and echo chambers, aligning with the dynamics of the Bounded Confidence Model and the Friedkin‑Johnsen model as measured by echo‑chamber indices.
Active and passive nudges are mitigation methods that reduce echo chambers in language‑based simulations.
ConTrans enables weak-to-strong alignment transfer via concept transplantation.
ConTrans refines concept vectors from a weakly aligned source LLM and reformulates them via affine transformation for a target LLM.
ConTrans transplants reformulated concept vectors into the residual stream of a target LLM.
ConTrans successfully transplants aligned concepts from 7B models to 13B and 70B models across multiple LLM families.
ConTrans surpasses instruction-tuned models in truthfulness.
ConTrans is effective for both inter-LLM-family and intra-LLM-family concept transplantation.
AdminBERT is a French pre‑trained language model for the administrative domain.
AdminSet is a pre‑training corpus of French administrative texts.
AdminSet‑NER is a named‑entity‑recognition dataset consisting exclusively of French administrative texts.
AdminBERT outperforms general‑domain language models, a large language model, and a non‑BERT architecture on NER tasks for French administrative texts.
GenWebNovel is a genre-oriented corpus for entity recognition in Chinese web novels covering the XuanHuan (Eastern Fantasy) and History genres.
GenWebNovel comprises 400 chapters totaling over 1.2 million tokens.

Analysis of GenWebNovel shows distinct distributions of person, location, and organization entities and different nesting patterns compared to the English literary corpus LitBank, indicating limited overlap across genres.
A novel method retrieves in-context learning examples with a pre-trained language model to boost entity recognition performance of large language models.
The in-context example retrieval method attains entity recognition performance comparable to state-of-the-art models without requiring additional training data.
Four prompting strategies—vanilla, role, candidate-based, and hybrid—are proposed and evaluated for zero-shot keyphrase extraction with large language models.
Hybrid prompting, which combines vanilla and candidate-based prompting, further enhances overall performance.
Role prompting, which assigns a task-related role to large language models, consistently improves performance across prompting strategies.
Llama3-8B-Instruct with vanilla prompting outperforms the state-of-the-art unsupervised method PromptRank on multiple keyphrase extraction benchmarks.
Llama3 and Gemma2 demonstrate the strongest zero-shot keyphrase extraction performance among evaluated large language models.
The impact of model size and different large language model series (GPT-4o, Gemma2, Qwen2) on zero-shot keyphrase extraction performance is analyzed.
Practical guidance for model selection in real-world zero-shot keyphrase extraction applications is provided.
Modal Feature Optimization Network (MFON) performs multimodal sentiment analysis using a Modal Prompt Attention mechanism to focus on under-optimized modalities.
MFON incorporates an intra-modal knowledge distillation strategy that preserves the integrity of modal features.
MFON applies inter-modal contrastive learning to better extract related features across modalities.
MFON achieves superior performance compared to existing state-of-the-art models on public benchmark datasets.
ACL-rlg is the largest open expert-annotated reading list dataset.
Multiple baselines for evaluating reading list generation are provided.
Reading list generation is formally defined as a retrieval task.
Traditional scholarly search engines and indexing methods perform poorly on reading list generation.
GPT-4o shows better results on reading list generation but exhibits signs of potential data contamination.
MPID is a modality-preserving and interaction-driven fusion network for multimodal sentiment analysis.
Token Refinement Module compresses representations of each modality.
Dual Perception Fusion Module integrates text and audio modalities.
Adaptive Graded Fusion Module integrates text and visual modalities.
Text representation is used to enhance the composite multimodal representation.
MPID achieves state-of-the-art performance on CMU-MOSI, CMU-MOSEI, and CH-SIMS datasets.
Thai-H6 benchmark assesses core capabilities such as reasoning, knowledge, and commonsense of Thai large language models.
Thai Cultural and Linguistic Intelligence Benchmark (ThaiCLI) evaluates cultural and linguistic understanding of Thai large language models.
Comprehensive evaluation of multilingual large language models using Thai-H6 and ThaiCLI provides analysis of how these benchmarks support Thai LLM development.
Datasets for Thai-H6 and ThaiCLI are released for public use.
The Direct-Indirect Reasoning (DIR) method treats direct reasoning and indirect reasoning as parallel paths that are merged to produce a final answer.
Prompt templates that incorporate contrapositive and contradiction principles stimulate large language models to perform indirect reasoning.
The DIR method can be straightforwardly integrated with existing chain-of-thought variants.
Combining DIR with various baseline methods yields significant performance improvements on logical reasoning and mathematical proof datasets.
INVP is a framework for investigating value priorities of large language models through decision-making in social scenarios.
INVP includes binary decision-making social scenarios covering individual and collective contexts and is based on Schwartz’s value theory.
A dataset of 1,613 social scenarios and 3,226 decisions across 283 topics is constructed using the INVP framework.
Evaluation of seven popular large language models on the dataset reveals higher priority for Universalism and Benevolence and lower priority for Power and Hedonism.
The analysis provides insights for enhancing moral and value alignment of large language models in complex social decisions.
Idea23D combines three large multimodal model agents for prompt generation, model selection, and feedback reflection in an automated collaborative loop.
Idea23D generates 3D models that align closely with multimodal input IDEAs composed of text, image, and 3D models.
Idea23D achieves higher success rate and accuracy in 3D generation compared to previous methods.
Eval3DAIGC-198 is a dataset of 198 multimodal inputs for evaluating alignment between generated 3D content and input IDEAs.
Idea23D demonstrates compatibility across various large multimodal models, text-to-image models, and image-to-3D models.
EthioEmo provides a multi-label emotion classification dataset for Amharic, Afan Oromo, Somali, and Tigrinya.
Extensive experiments evaluate encoder-only, encoder-decoder, and decoder-only language models on multi-label emotion classification for Ethiopian languages and English.
Zero-shot and few-shot large language model approaches are compared to fine-tuned smaller language models for multi-label emotion classification.
Accurate multi-label emotion classification is found to be insufficient even for high-resource languages such as English.
Performance of multi-label emotion classification varies across languages and model architectures, revealing a large gap between high-resource and low-resource languages.
A model that integrates structural information extracted from large language models with surprisal statistics is introduced.
The model achieves higher correlation with human reading times than surprisal-based models on garden path sentences.
The model provides better prediction of the garden path effect than surprisal-based models.
The model can distinguish between sentence types with different levels of difficulty.
A flexible paradigm for individual preference alignment disentangles preference representation from text generation in large language models.
The paradigm achieves aligned generation quality comparable to or better than parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
The paradigm reduces the additional training time required for adapting to new individual preferences compared to parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
The paradigm is evaluated on multiple text generation tasks.
FuncEvalGMN is a graph-based metric for evaluating functional correctness of SQL queries in Text-to-SQL tasks.
FuncEvalGMN extracts semantic information from logical execution plans using a relational operator tree (ROT) called RelNode and embeds it into a graph.
FuncEvalGMN trains a graph neural network to perform graph matching on pairs of SQL queries via graph contrastive learning.
FuncEvalGMN requires only the database schema to derive logical execution plans, eliminating the need for test database preparation.
FuncEvalGMN generalizes strongly to unseen datasets, demonstrating robustness as a functional correctness metric across diverse Text-to-SQL applications.
A parameter-efficient fine-tuning method combines deconvolution with subspace learning.
The method reduces the number of parameters required for fine-tuning by an order of magnitude.
The method achieves superior training efficiency compared to existing models.
The method achieves superior performance compared to existing models.
A Fusion Selection-Generation-Based Approach combines selective methods and generative methods for event argument extraction.
The approach employs fusion prompts, element-based extraction, and fusion learning to integrate selective and generative characteristics.
The approach addresses challenges of input, process, and output fusion in event argument extraction.
The approach demonstrates state-of-the-art performance and efficiency on the RAMS and WikiEvents datasets.
Five contamination detection approaches are evaluated on four state-of-the-art large language models across eight challenging datasets.
Current contamination detection methods exhibit non-trivial limitations in their assumptions and practical applications.
Detecting contamination introduced during instruction fine-tuning with answer augmentation is notably difficult.
Contamination detection techniques show limited consistency with each other.
Multi-view Heterogeneous Graph enhances relation path extraction from different mentioned entities.
Self-optimizing causal intervention model generates assistant entities to mitigate out-of-distribution problems caused by counterfactual relations.
Grounding method extracts evidence graphs from the knowledge graph using faithful inference chains.
The proposed model provides precise evidence graphs for fact verification.
The proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance on a public KG-based fact verification dataset.
A conditional contrastive learning framework is introduced for conditional semantic textual similarity.
The framework adaptively selects the optimization direction for positive and negative samples to resolve over-estimation of similarity in conditional semantic textual similarity.
The framework fully balances the effects of hard and false negative samples in conditional semantic textual similarity.
The method achieves state-of-the-art performance on conditional semantic textual similarity across five bi-encoder and tri-encoder models.
Multi-turn Medical Dialogue Evaluation (MMD-Eval) is a benchmark for evaluating proactive communication and diagnostic capabilities of medical large language models during consultations.
MMD-Eval addresses the limitation of single-turn question-answering tasks for assessing medical consultation abilities of large language models.
A task-oriented dialogue system was trained to simulate patients using a structured medical records dataset, generating multi-turn dialogue data for evaluation.
The benchmark evaluates the communication skills and medical expertise of medical large language models.
LegalHalBench is a benchmark for evaluating hallucinations in legal question answering.
Three automatic metrics are introduced to assess common hallucinations in legal question answering.
A hallucination mitigation method combines behavior cloning with Hard Sample-aware Iterative Direct Preference Optimization (HIPO).
The hallucination mitigation method improves Non-Hallucinated Statute Rate, Statute Relevance Rate, and Legal Claim Truthfulness in legal question answering.
Extensive real-data experiments demonstrate the effectiveness of the hallucination mitigation method.
TMATH is a dataset for evaluating large language models' ability to generate educational hints for math word problems.
TMATH contains diverse math word problems paired with human-generated hints.
Fine-tuning 7B-scale language models on TMATH improves their generation of accurate and contextually appropriate educational hints for math word problems.
Large language models still encounter difficulties with complex reasoning when generating hints for math word problems.
ELAINE-medLLM is a trilingual (English, Japanese, Chinese) large language model adapted for the biomedical domain and built on Llama-3-8B.

A curated multilingual biomedical training dataset with high volume and diversity is used to adapt the model to the biomedical domain and to endow trilingual capability while preserving the base model's knowledge.
Training follows a two-stage process consisting of continued pre-training followed by supervised fine-tuning.
ELAINE-medLLM demonstrates superior trilingual capabilities compared to existing bilingual or multilingual medical large language models without severely sacrificing the base model's capabilities.
QUENCH is a text-based English quizzing benchmark manually curated and transcribed from YouTube quiz videos.
QUENCH includes masked entities and rationales for large language models to predict via generation.
QUENCH assesses world knowledge and deduction capabilities of large language models in a zero-shot, open-domain quizzing setup.
An extensive evaluation of multiple large language models reveals the influence of model size, prompting style, geographical context, and gold-labeled rationale generation on performance.
An error analysis identifies various types of generative errors that large language models commonly produce in the QUENCH benchmark.
Emotional Knowledge Tool Calling (EKTC) framework encapsulates commonsense knowledge bases as empathetic tools for flexible external knowledge integration via tool calling.
TOOL-ED dataset is a novel dataset constructed based on EMPATHETICDIALOGUE for empathetic response generation with tool calling.
EKTC enhances the ability of large language models to generate empathetic responses on the EMPATHETICDIALOGUE dataset.
A strategy-enhanced role-playing framework simulates authentic emotional support conversations using three roles: Seeker, Strategy Counselor, and Supporter.
The ServeForEmo dataset provides a large collection of multi-turn dialogues for training emotional support agents.
SweetieChat is an emotional support agent trained on the ServeForEmo dataset that can handle diverse open-domain scenarios.
Experiments and human evaluations demonstrate that the strategy-enhanced role-playing framework yields more nuanced and tailored emotional support.
MBTIBench is a manually annotated MBTI personality detection dataset with soft labels, created under psychologist guidance.
Soft labels in MBTI personality detection provide greater benefits to other psychological tasks than hard labels.
Large language models exhibit polarized predictions and biases in MBTI personality detection.
Logical and dialectical quality dimensions are separated more clearly, with distinct definitions and annotation granularity.
Methodological considerations from argument theory improve state-of-the-art computational argument quality assessment methods.
Dialectical considerations have become more central than logical perspectives in computational argument quality assessment, as evidenced by analysis of prior empirical work.
DynRank enhances passage retrieval in open-domain question answering via dynamic zero-shot question classification.
A dynamic prompting mechanism generates contextually relevant prompts based on fine-grained question type classifications.
A pre-trained question classification model is leveraged to categorize questions into fine-grained types for zero-shot prompting.
DynRank is integrated into existing retrieval frameworks to improve their adaptability across different questions and contexts.
Extensive experiments on multiple QA benchmark datasets demonstrate that DynRank improves passage retrieval performance.
A new evaluation metric categorizes ASR transcription errors into four severity levels with subtypes based on linguistic criteria, contextual patterns, and content-word analysis.
The new metric is applied to evaluate ten state-of-the-art French ASR systems, including HMM-based and end-to-end models.
The evaluation identifies strengths and weaknesses of each ASR system and determines which systems provide the most comfortable reading experience for users.
Evolution Strategy Optimization (ESO) is an on-policy post fine-tuning method for autoregressive language models.
ESO treats model tuning as an evolution process where each generated sentence provides a perturbation signal to the model parameter space.
ESO quantifies the fitness of perturbation signals by the difference between the signal's reward score and the average reward.
ESO achieves superior performance across multiple tasks compared to existing post fine-tuning methods.
ESO attains comparable performance on human alignment tasks.
Reference-focused synthetic APE data generation uses reference sentences to produce corrupted translations.
APE systems trained with reference-focused synthetic data outperform systems trained with existing synthetic data.
Combining existing synthetic data with reference-focused synthetic data yields additional performance gains for APE systems.
Reference-focused synthetic APE data is released as a publicly available resource.
MLaKE provides a benchmark of multi-hop and single-hop questions for evaluating multilingual knowledge editing across five languages.
MLaKE aggregates fact chains from Wikipedia and uses large language models to generate questions and answers.
Current multilingual knowledge editing methods exhibit considerable inconsistencies in multilingual performance and encoding efficiency, limiting their ability to generalize across languages.
Multilingual knowledge editing methods achieve higher accuracy when editing English compared to other languages.
Models encode knowledge and generation capabilities for different languages in distinct parameters, resulting in poor cross-lingual transfer performance for current methods.
Transfer performance of multilingual knowledge editing methods is better within the same language family than across different families.
KVFKT integrates attention-based embedding of questions with attention vectors over knowledge concepts during answer and timestamp collection.
KVFKT incorporates a forgetting quantification module that updates the student knowledge state matrix before prediction using interval time and forgetting rate derived from the forgetting curve.
KVFKT's answer prediction module generates responses based on the student's knowledge status, a guess coefficient, and question difficulty.
KVFKT includes a knowledge status update module that refines the student's knowledge state according to their answers and question characteristics.
KVFKT achieves superior knowledge tracing performance compared to state-of-the-art models on multiple real-world datasets.
Benchmark results for Gemma2, LLaMA3, and ChatGPT on cryptic crossword solving are significantly below human performance.
An analysis identifies factors that cause LLMs to struggle with cryptic crossword solving.
New cryptic crossword datasets are released for research.
Code for reproducing cryptic crossword experiments is released.
KriRAG is a Retrieval-Augmented Generation architecture that structures and summarizes extensive document collections based on investigative queries.
KriRAG provides relevant document references and detailed answers for each investigative query.
A comprehensive evaluation methodology for retrieval-augmented generation in criminal investigations is established, incorporating semantic retrieval, scoring, reasoning, and query response accuracy.
KriRAG achieves high accuracy in relevance assessment and query response when evaluated against queries and answers provided by criminal investigators.
KriRAG is designed to run offline on limited hardware, enabling on-device processing of sensitive data.

The data quality enhancement method selects data with a greedy algorithm, divides the dataset into sampled and unsampled subsets, fine-tunes a large language model on the sampled data, and categorizes incorrectly predicted unsampled data into uncovered, difficult, and noisy categories.
The data quality enhancement method improves the performance of large language models on text classification tasks.
The data quality enhancement method significantly reduces training time for large language models on text classification tasks.
The data quality enhancement method achieves state-of-the-art performance on several open-source text classification tasks.
A benchmark evaluates the robustness of reference-free dialogue evaluation metrics against speaker tag prefix, static response, ungrammatical response, and repeated conversational context attacks.
The benchmark assesses DialogRPT, UniEval, and PromptEval on both grounded and ungrounded dialogue datasets.
Correlation with human judgment and susceptibility to adversarial attacks are not always aligned for reference-free dialogue evaluation metrics.
Metrics that appear equivalent on traditional benchmarks can assign different scores to adversarial responses.
MURRE performs multi-hop table retrieval with removal for open-domain text-to-SQL.
MURRE removes previously retrieved information from the question to guide the retriever toward unretrieved relevant tables.
MURRE achieves average improvement over previous state-of-the-art results on two open-domain text-to-SQL datasets.
Attention-Seeker uses self-attention maps from a large language model to score candidate keyphrases.
Attention-Seeker identifies layers, heads, and attention vectors that focus on the key topics of a document.
Attention-Seeker dynamically adapts to input text without manual parameter tuning.
Attention-Seeker outperforms most baseline unsupervised keyphrase extraction models on four public datasets.
Attention-Seeker achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of evaluated datasets, particularly for long documents.
A Bayesian evaluation framework is proposed to comprehensively assess large language models' function modeling abilities.
Large language models are relatively weak in understanding patterns in raw data.
Large language models excel at utilizing prior domain knowledge to develop a strong understanding of the underlying function.
New insights about the strengths and limitations of large language models in function modeling are provided.
BLIND-VALM augments a language model with visually‑grounded text representations from CLIP, eliminating the need for explicit images.
BLIND-VALM matches VALM performance on visual language understanding, natural language understanding, and language modeling tasks while being more efficient and simpler.
Scaling up BLIND-VALM within VALM's compute budget, by increasing model size or pre‑training corpus, yields superior performance to VALM on all evaluation tasks.
Explicit images are not necessary to visually augment a language model.
A probing classifier that inputs embeddings from the last hidden layer of Vision Language Models performs multi-class veracity classification for fact-checking.
Fusing embeddings extracted separately from text and image encoders yields superior fact-checking performance compared to using embeddings from integrated Vision Language Models.
Incorporating multimodal content improves fact-checking performance relative to text-only models.
The neural probing classifier outperforms KNN and SVM baselines when leveraging extracted embeddings for multimodal fact-checking.
Experiments on two fact-checking datasets validate the effectiveness of the probing classifier and multimodal embedding fusion.
A regression analysis method isolates the effects of nuisance factors and datasets from model capability effects to explain variability in NLP test scores.
The regression analysis method is applied to a case study of popular biomedical NLP models.

In the biomedical NLP case study, the performance difference between BioLinkBERT and MSR BiomedBERT is found to be substantially smaller than previously reported after accounting for nuisance factors and datasets.
RAM2C framework generates HTS‑compliant educational dialogues by retrieving from domain‑specific knowledge bases and coordinating multiple LLMs with distinct roles.
HTS‑guided knowledge bases covering teaching skills, psychology, and safety ethics are constructed.
LLMs fine‑tuned on the RAM2C‑generated dataset exhibit superior performance in Chinese reading teaching, delivering more personalized and ethically safe teaching responses.
Empirical evaluation confirms the practicality and high quality of the educational dialogues produced by RAM2C.

MAC-SQL is a multi-agent collaborative framework for Text-to-SQL that combines a core decomposer agent using few-shot chain-of-thought reasoning with auxiliary agents that employ external tools to acquire sub-databases and refine erroneous SQL queries.
SQL-Llama, an open-source instruction-followed model fine-tuned from Code Llama 7B, can perform all Text-to-SQL tasks in the MAC-SQL framework with execution accuracy comparable to GPT-4.
MAC-SQL combined with GPT-4 establishes a new state-of-the-art execution accuracy on the BIRD benchmark holdout test set.
WIKIGENBENCH is a benchmark of 1,320 entries for generating and evaluating full-length Wikipedia articles in real-world scenarios.
WIKIGENBENCH incorporates systematic metrics and LLM-based metrics to assess verifiability, organization, and other aspects of generated Wikipedia content.

Experiments compare direct retrieval-augmented generation, hierarchical structure-based retrieval-augmented generation, and retrieval-augmented generation with a fine-tuned generation model on WIKIGENBENCH.
Hierarchical structure-based retrieval-augmented generation produces more comprehensive Wikipedia content than the other evaluated methods.
Fine-tuned generation models achieve higher verifiability of generated Wikipedia articles than the other evaluated methods.
ECC leverages specialized encoders to capture emotion, cause, and commonsense features.
ECC models emotion, cause, and commonsense jointly through a Conditional Variational Auto-Encoder.
ECC employs novel loss functions to refine the interplay among emotion, cause, and commonsense.
ECC generates empathetic responses using an energy-based model with ODE sampling.
ECC outperforms existing baselines on the EmpatheticDialogues dataset for empathetic dialogue generation.
TransMI creates strong baselines for transliterated data by leveraging an existing multilingual pretrained language model and its tokenizer without any training.

TransMI transliterates the vocabulary of a multilingual pretrained language model into a common script, merges the new vocabulary with the original vocabulary, and initializes embeddings of the new subwords.
TransMI preserves the multilingual pretrained language model’s ability to handle non-transliterated data.
TransMI enables a multilingual pretrained language model to effectively process transliterated data, facilitating cross-lingual transfer across scripts.
PADO is a multi-agent framework that detects the Big Five OCEAN personality traits in human-generated texts using large language models.
PADO employs personality-induced agents that analyze text from multiple perspectives and uses a comparative judgment process to determine personality trait levels.
The proposed approach compares contrasting perspectives to capture the relative nature of personality traits.
Experiments demonstrate that PADO is effective and generalizable across various large language models, including smaller parameter models.
PADO provides a more nuanced, context-aware method for personality detection.
BoFiT is a fine-grained human motion video captioning dataset for the body fitness training scenario.
BoFiT-Gen generates fine-grained descriptions of human motions by extracting angular representations with computer vision techniques and prompting large language models.
BoFiT-Gen outperforms previous video captioning methods on comprehensive evaluation metrics.
BoFiT serves as an evaluation set for visio‑linguistic models of human motion.
Quantizes all parameters of Mamba-2 to sub-2-bit precision using BitNet quantization across embedding, projection, and linear layers.
Pre-trains the fully quantized Mamba-2 model from scratch on a large-scale token corpus.
Achieves a substantially larger reduction in bits used by all parameters than conventional BitNet quantization.
Shows minimal performance degradation in pre-training perplexity and downstream tasks despite extreme quantization.
BPE-based subword segmentation impairs large language models' ability to handle certain affixations.
Large language models perform poorly at generating novel word forms involving affixations because subwords are marked as initial or intra-word.
Increasing model size and training data mitigates the impairment by aligning initial- and intra-word embeddings.
Consistent example selection in in-context learning improves handling of affixations by large language models.
Morphological segmentation achieves near‑perfect accuracy in generating novel word forms, outperforming BPE-based segmentation.
ELITR-Bench evaluates long-context language models on a meeting assistant scenario using noisy automatic speech recognition transcripts.
ELITR-Bench augments the ELITR corpus with 271 manually crafted questions and corresponding ground-truth answers.
ELITR-Bench includes noisy versions of meeting transcripts altered to target different word error rate levels.
Successive generations of proprietary and open long-context language models exhibit performance improvements on ELITR-Bench.
Long-context language models differ in robustness to transcript noise on ELITR-Bench.
GPT-4 scores align with human judges in the evaluation of ELITR-Bench.
GPT-4's ability to distinguish beyond three score levels is limited in the evaluation of ELITR-Bench.
Symbol-to-language (S2L) converts symbol-based representations into language-based representations for large language model reasoning.
Incorporating S2L substantially enhances the accuracy of large language models on 1D-ARC and Dyck language tasks.
S2L yields consistent improvements across multiple general symbol-related tasks, including table understanding and Tweet analysis.
Both closed-source and open-source large language models exhibit large performance gains on symbol-related problems when using S2L.
PDC-CSER generates pseudo-labels for augmented Chinese semantic error recognition samples based on perplexity and model predictions.
CSER-DSA incorporates a dependency syntactic attention mechanism during fine-tuning to infuse dependency syntactic information.
CSER-DSA reduces computational resources and time cost while maintaining robust performance on Chinese semantic error recognition.
PDC-CSER and CSER-DSA achieve superior performance compared to existing models on Chinese semantic error recognition.

MQM-APE is a universal training-free framework that filters non-impactful errors by automatically post-editing translations based on each error, retaining only errors that contribute to quality improvement.
MQM-APE employs large language models as an evaluator to provide error annotations, as a post-editor to assess error impact, and as a pairwise quality verifier to filter errors.
MQM-APE consistently improves the reliability and quality of error spans compared to GEMBA-MQM across eight large language models in both high- and low-resource languages.
MQM-APE complements translation-specific evaluators such as Tower, demonstrating broad applicability.
Analysis confirms the effectiveness of each MQM-APE module and provides insights into evaluator design and large language model selection.
GroUSE is a meta-evaluation benchmark comprising 144 unit tests for assessing the calibration and discrimination of judge models in grounded question answering.
Seven distinct generator failure modes for grounded answer generation are identified.
A novel pipeline is introduced to improve automated evaluation of retrieval-augmented generation systems.
Closed models achieve strong performance on GroUSE.
State-of-the-art open-source judges fail to generalize to the proposed evaluation criteria on GroUSE.
Finetuning Llama-3 on GPT-4 reasoning traces markedly improves its evaluation capabilities, enhancing both correlation with GPT-4 judgments and calibration on reference situations.
Current automated RAG evaluation frameworks often overlook important failure modes even when employing GPT-4 as a judge.
Generative information extraction methods based on pre-trained language models and large language models are reviewed with emphasis on adaptation and generalization capabilities.
The relationship between generative information extraction methods and their adaptation and generalization aspects is discussed.
Model collaboration is highlighted as a strategy to balance task performance with computational demands of large language models.
Approaches for integrating diverse information extraction tasks into unified models are explored.
BasqBBQ is a benchmark that assesses social biases in Basque across eight domains using a multiple-choice question-answering task.
BasqBBQ evaluates the accuracy and bias transmission of autoregressive large language models, including multilingual and Basque-adapted models.
Larger autoregressive language models achieve higher accuracy on the BasqBBQ benchmark.
Larger autoregressive language models exhibit lower negative bias on the BasqBBQ benchmark.
High negative bias persists in the categories Disability Status, Age, and Physical Appearance, especially in ambiguous contexts, on the BasqBBQ benchmark.
The categories Sexual Orientation, Gender Identity, and Race/Ethnicity exhibit the least bias in ambiguous contexts on the BasqBBQ benchmark.
Continual pre-training based adaptation for Basque has limited impact on bias compared with English.
Hyperbolic label-aware loss functions achieve the best performance on low-level labels in hierarchical multi-label text classification.
Hyperbolic label-aware loss functions compromise overall consistency and micro-averaged performance in hierarchical multi-label text classification.
Contrastive loss functions are less effective for hierarchical multi-label text classification when implemented in hyperbolic space compared to Euclidean space.
Hyperbolic space does not provide additional benefits over Euclidean space for hierarchical multi-label text classification when appropriate metrics and training objectives are used.
CateEA combines implicit entity category information with multi-modal representations for entity alignment.
CateEA generates pseudo-category labels from entity embeddings.
CateEA integrates pseudo-category labels into a multi-task learning framework.
CateEA enables adaptive adjustment of similarity measures to improve alignment precision and robustness in multi-modal contexts.
CateEA outperforms state-of-the-art methods on benchmark datasets for multi-modal entity alignment.
DAEA is an entity alignment approach that uses domain adaptation to improve performance on real-world knowledge graphs.
DAEA introduces a multi-source knowledge graph selection mechanism for entity alignment.
DAEA introduces a domain-adaptive entity alignment loss function to bridge the gap between synthetic benchmark data and real-world data.
DAEA achieves higher Hits@1 on real-world datasets DOREMUS and AGROLD than state-of-the-art models.
LLM agents can understand probability distributions in sequential decision-making tasks.
LLM agents struggle with probabilistic sampling when generating action sequences.
Integrating coding tools improves LLM agents' probabilistic sampling ability to some extent.
LLM agents' sampling precision remains insufficient for accurately simulating human behavior as agents, even with coding tool integration.
A framework evaluates LLMs on sequence simulation with explicit probability distributions and on sequence simulation with implicit probability distributions.
Sequential Fusion of Text-close and Text-far Representations (SFTTR) refines multimodal representations by incorporating both text-close and text-far information.
SFTTR employs contrastive learning to explore similarities and differences between text and audio/visual modalities.
SFTTR includes a sequential cross-modal encoder that sequentially fuses representations that are close to and far from the text modality.
LoRA-drop evaluates the importance of LoRA parameters using their output values.
LoRA-drop retains LoRA parameters for layers deemed important and shares a single LoRA across the remaining layers.
LoRA-drop attains performance comparable to full fine-tuning and standard LoRA while using approximately 50% of the LoRA parameters.
LoRA-drop is evaluated on models of various scales across natural language understanding and generation tasks.
Small Llama-based models trained with character-level vocabularies achieve strong linguistic performance on standard syntactic benchmarks.
Small Llama-based models trained with character-level vocabularies achieve strong linguistic performance on novel lexical and phonetic benchmarks.
Phoneme-based models almost match grapheme-based models in both standard tasks and novel evaluations.
Adapters Selector trains a middleman adapter that selects the appropriate LoRA adapter for a given input based on its task and domain.
Adapters Selector enables cross-domain multi-task inference using a compact model combined with multiple LoRA modules.
ModaFact provides joint factuality and modality annotations for event-denoting expressions in Italian.
ModaFact introduces a novel annotation scheme for joint factuality and modality that aligns with existing schemes.
Various classification systems are evaluated on ModaFact as a preliminary step toward using factuality and modality information in downstream tasks.
The ModaFact dataset and its best-performing classification model are released publicly under an open license.
Grapheme Pair Encoding (GPE) incorporates graphemes into the Byte Pair Encoding algorithm.
Pre-tokenization choices have a larger impact than tokenization algorithms on achieving egalitarian language representation in large language models.
Grapheme-based character extraction outperforms byte-level tokenizers for complex scripts such as Tamil, Sinhala, and Hindi.
Experiments on Tamil, Sinhala, and Hindi validate that GPE provides more egalitarian representation than existing tokenizers.
MIT-10M is a large-scale parallel corpus of multilingual image translation containing millions of image-text pairs.
MIT-10M includes images in three sizes, 28 categories, three difficulty levels, and multilingual annotations in 14 languages.
MIT-10M incorporates extensive data cleaning and multilingual translation validation.
Models trained on MIT-10M exhibit higher adaptability for evaluating performance on challenging real-world image translation tasks.
Fine-tuning a model with MIT-10M yields substantially higher performance than a baseline model.
Summarizing long answers before evaluation improves lexical matching and semantic metric evaluation results.
A multi-layered evaluation methodology that combines different metrics tailored to various scenarios is introduced.
Combining simple metrics achieves performance comparable to large language models as judges while reducing evaluation cost.
A fused approach that integrates lexical and semantic metrics with large language models through a specific formula outperforms previous evaluation solutions.
A taxonomy-embedded transitional LLM-agnostic framework for multimodal hierarchical classification is proposed.
The framework enforces consistency across hierarchical levels in classification predictions.
Evaluations on the MEP-3M multimodal e-commerce product dataset demonstrate that the framework outperforms conventional LLM structures for hierarchical classification.
CLOB enables continual learning for large language models using only verbal prompting without fine-tuning or adding trainable parameters.
CLOB treats the large language model as a black box, making it suitable for models accessed via APIs.
CIS is a continual learning technique based on incremental summarization that overcomes the input length limit of large language models.
CIS achieves superior performance compared to baseline continual learning methods.
CHIFRAUD is a long-term dataset of Chinese fraudulent texts collected over 12 months from billions of web pages.
A comprehensive set of baseline detectors, including large language model-based and pre-trained language model approaches, is provided for evaluating Chinese fraud detection.
A ground-truth dataset of social media posts about vaccines and immigration manually labeled with tropes is created.
The task of automatic trope detection in short social media texts is formally defined and distinguished from prior work.
A supervised multi-label classification technique for detecting tropes in short texts is developed.
Fine-tuned models can detect tropes in social media posts with high accuracy.
Tropes are found to be common across domains in social media discussions.
SVD-GCL integrates truncated singular value decomposition in the feature engineering stage to reduce dimensionality and denoise data.
SVD-GCL employs noise augmentation by gradually adding noise to embeddings during training to enrich data samples.
SVD-GCL extracts self-supervised signals from graph data for contrastive learning.
SVD-GCL alleviates data sparsity in high-dimensional, sparse recommendation datasets.
SVD-GCL maintains stable performance in the presence of noise.
SVD-GCL offers computational efficiency, higher recommendation performance, and increased robustness compared to prior GNN-based recommender models.
A framework with three modules assesses diversity, redundancy, and difficulty of data attributes in LLM evaluation benchmarks.
A progressive incorporation method analyzes the influence of diversity, redundancy, and difficulty on benchmark rankings.
LLM rankings on a revised benchmark that accounts for diversity, redundancy, and difficulty correlate meaningfully with rankings on the original benchmark.
Existing evaluation benchmarks for LLMs often fail to satisfy diversity, redundancy, and difficulty criteria, indicating insufficient consideration of multifaceted data attributes.
CLSC performs joint machine translation and sentence compression with length constraints.
Multiple Models (MM) trains separate models for each compression ratio.
Controllable Model (CM) uses a compression token to encode length constraints in a single model per language.
Evaluation on subtitle data and EuroParl transcriptions shows that the models preserve high semantic meaning in compressed outputs.
Constraining data sampling enables effective training of the models in low-resource settings for multiple languages.
HYDEN learns image-text representations as hyperbolic density embeddings for medical data.
HYDEN integrates text-aware local features with global image features and maps them to hyperbolic pseudo-Gaussian distributions.
HYDEN employs an encapsulation loss function to model partial order relations between image-text density distributions.
HYDEN produces interpretable image-text representations.
HYDEN achieves superior performance compared to baseline methods on zero-shot and fine-tuning tasks across multiple datasets.
PropaInsight provides a conceptual framework that systematically dissects propaganda into techniques, arousal appeals, and underlying intent.
PropaGaze is a novel dataset that combines human-annotated data with high-quality synthetic data generated through a meticulously designed pipeline.
Off-the-shelf large language models perform poorly on propaganda analysis tasks.
Fine-tuned Llama-7B-Chat achieves substantially higher technique identification accuracy and appeal analysis quality than 1-shot GPT-4-Turbo.
PropaGaze enables effective propaganda analysis in data-sparse and cross-domain scenarios by complementing limited human-annotated data.
A method creates parallel sense-annotated datasets for any language that can be translated from English by combining machine translation, word alignment, sense projection, and sense filtering.
The method generates silver annotations that can be manually revised to produce gold sense-annotated datasets.
New parallel benchmark datasets for Farsi, Chinese, and Bengali are produced and vetted by native speakers.
Automatically-generated silver datasets exhibit higher quality than annotations from recent multilingual word‑sense disambiguation systems, particularly for non‑European languages.
XFormParser unifies semantic entity recognition and relation extraction within a single multimodal multilingual form parsing framework.
XFormParser incorporates a comprehensive transformer-based pre-trained language model and a Bi-LSTM component to enhance multilingual parsing performance.
InDFormSFT is a supervised fine-tuning dataset designed for industrial form parsing across diverse contexts.
XFormParser attains higher relation extraction performance than prior state-of-the-art models in language-specific settings.
XFormParser delivers superior cross-task performance in multilingual and zero-shot form parsing scenarios.
Pre-trained audio representations from Wav2Vec and Whisper are employed for cross-lingual abusive language detection in low-resource Indian languages.
Model-Agnostic Meta-Learning (MAML) is integrated with these audio representations to classify abusive language across ten languages.
Few-shot learning experiments using limited data (e.g., 50 to 200 examples) evaluate the impact of shot size on detection performance.
Feature visualization analysis is conducted to provide insights into model behavior for cross-lingual audio abuse detection.
Pre-trained audio models demonstrate generalization ability for abusive language detection in low-resource multilingual settings.
A Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback based fine-tuning framework for sexism detection is introduced.
The framework is evaluated on Mistral-7B and LLaMA-3-8B across zero-shot, supervised fine-tuning, and RLHF scenarios.
Experimental results demonstrate that RLHF enhances explainable sexism detection performance.
LLaMA-3-8B fine-tuned with RLHF achieves the best performance among the evaluated models on the Explainable Detection of Online Sexism benchmark.
HiWikiEdits is a high-quality Hindi grammar error correction dataset containing 8,137 human-edited Wikipedia instances annotated with 17 grammatical error types using the ERRANT toolkit.
Using English as a pivot language in round‑trip translation generates synthetic Hindi GEC data that yields higher GLEU scores than using Assamese or Marathi as pivots.
A neural model‑based synthetic error‑generation technique achieves performance comparable to other synthetic data generation methods for Hindi GEC in low‑resource settings.
TEXT-CAKE is an evaluation benchmark for assessing local text coherence across four languages and four text genres.
Encoder-based multilingual and monolingual language models with varying architectures and parameters are systematically evaluated under different finetuning settings for local coherence detection.
Detecting subtle perturbations that disrupt local coherence remains challenging for encoder-based language models.
Incorporating diverse text genres during pre-training, using an optimal pretraining objective, and employing a large vocabulary size enhance models' ability to detect local coherence.
Deep encoder-based language models with more layers outperform shallower models in local coherence detection, even when they have fewer total parameters.
Large language models are fine-tuned to predict morphosyntactic annotations for Torlak.
Several predictive models are compared for morphosyntactic annotation prediction in Torlak.
Large language models are fine-tuned to score the degree of “Torlakness” of a sentence by labeling likely Torlak tokens.
The Torlakness scoring model facilitates documentation of additional Torlak transcribed speech with non‑standard features.
MuPe Life Stories Dataset provides 289 life story interviews (365 hours) in Brazilian Portuguese with speakers diverse in age, education, and regional accents.
Current Brazilian Portuguese automatic speech recognition models exhibit bias with respect to speaker education levels and age groups.
Training on MuPe Life Stories Dataset mitigates education-level and age-group bias in Brazilian Portuguese automatic speech recognition models.
An automatic speech recognition model trained on MuPe Life Stories Dataset achieves improved performance on a diverse test set compared to models trained without it.
An automatic speech recognition model trained on MuPe Life Stories Dataset can be extrinsically evaluated through a topic modeling task using its automatically transcribed output.
LaERC-S is a framework that enables large language models to incorporate speaker characteristics, including mental state and behavior, for emotion recognition in conversation.
LaERC-S employs a two-stage learning process that first reasons about speaker characteristics and then tracks speaker emotions in complex conversation scenarios.
LaERC-S achieves state-of-the-art performance on three benchmark emotion recognition in conversation datasets.
A novel dataset of metaphoric analogies in literary texts is constructed with domain expert assistance.
The task of extracting the concepts that form metaphoric analogies from literary texts is formulated.
Recent large language models are evaluated on structuring metaphoric mappings from text fragments containing explicit proportional analogies.
Recent large language models are evaluated on generating implicit elements of metaphoric analogies that are indirectly suggested in texts.
LLMs achieve competitive performance on extracting metaphoric analogies, demonstrating the feasibility of automatic extraction without manual labeling.
Prompting a large language model with multimodal source content and the corresponding response set extracts contrasting evidence for rumor detection.
An uncertainty-aware evidential evaluator assesses evidence intensity from multimodal source content and dual-sided reasoning to derive final predictions.
Modeling second-order probability enables indication of prediction uncertainty (reliability).
Reasoning from the correct perspective yields natural language explanations for predictions.
Experiments demonstrate that the method outperforms contemporary LLM-based rumor detection approaches in effectiveness, uncertainty awareness, explainability, and efficiency.
A set of 18 readability metrics covering statistical, part-of-speech, syntactic, semantic, and fluency aspects is compiled for evaluating German medical texts.
The suitability of these metrics for measuring readability of German medical texts is assessed through statistical testing on technical abstracts and simplified summaries.
Semantic and fluency readability metrics are shown to transfer successfully to German medical texts.
Several statistical, part-of-speech-based, and syntactic readability metrics exhibit different behavior when applied to German medical texts, indicating the need for careful interpretation.
Goal-oriented game self-play is introduced as an evaluation paradigm for multimodal models.
Games are defined that test a model’s ability to represent visual situations and align representations through dialogue.
The largest closed multimodal models achieve strong performance on the defined games.
The best open-weight multimodal models struggle on the defined games.
Exceptional deep captioning capabilities of the largest models contribute to their performance on the games.
Speech representation is conceptualized as a combination of content-agnostic and content-relevant factors.
Introducing content-agnostic perturbations to speech signals causes substantial degradation in speech translation performance.
SRPSE framework removes content-agnostic components from speech representations to mitigate their negative impact on speech translation.
SRPSE improves translation performance across all directions in multiple settings on the MuST-C and CoVoST-2 datasets.
SRPSE achieves state-of-the-art performance under a transcript-free setting.
A clustering-based active learning framework enhanced with knowledge distillation enables effective active learning for generative tasks by transforming intermediate outputs of a learner model.
The framework produces more representative language models without requiring prior knowledge of the data distribution or extensive human effort.
The framework is validated through case studies in counter-narration and style transfer.
Two new datasets for inclusive generation are constructed alongside model training.
Models trained with the framework achieve higher performance than baseline models.
Models trained with the framework exhibit more consistent performance across data subgroups and increased lexical diversity, indicating resilience to data skewness.
Data acquired by the framework improves the performance of secondary models that are not part of the learning loop.
ConGen provides a collection of naturally occurring generic and quantified sentences in context.
p-acceptability is a surprisal-based metric that captures quantification sensitivity.
Generics exhibit greater context sensitivity than determiner quantifiers.
A notable portion of naturally occurring generics express weak generalisations.
Language models reveal human stereotype biases present in generic statements.
LASS uses both large and small language models to generate synthetic data for debiasing opinion summarization.
LASS synthesizes negative reviews by rewriting positive texts with a large language model.
LASS trains a disentangle reconstruction model on the generated data to enable further synthetic data generation.

LASS generates large amounts of synthetic data by decoding new representations obtained from combining different sample representations and filtering them based on perplexity and sentiment classification.
Experiments demonstrate that LASS alleviates emotional bias in opinion summarization comparably to using only large models while being more economical.
A syntax-aware sequence-to-sequence pre-trained model for sequence-to-sequence-based relation extraction is introduced.
The model incorporates dependency information into a sequence-to-sequence pre-trained language model through continual pre-training on a sequence-to-sequence dependency parsing task.
Dependency parsing pre-training improves relation extraction performance on two widely used benchmark datasets.
Providing highly specific, instructive codebooks to quantized large language models improves zero-shot classification performance on complex multilabel frame datasets.
Codebook-based zero-shot classification can outperform few-shot in-context learning setups on the evaluated datasets.
Codebook-based zero-shot classification is equally or more token-efficient than few-shot in-context learning.
Codebook-based zero-shot classification requires less hands-on engineering than few-shot in-context learning.
The largest English and Chinese financial entity-level sentiment analysis datasets to date are constructed.
Self-aware In-context Learning Correction (SILC) introduces a two-stage sentiment analysis pipeline for financial entity-level sentiment analysis.
The first stage of SILC fine-tunes a base large language model to generate pseudo-labeled data specific to financial entity-level sentiment analysis.
The second stage of SILC trains a correction model using a graph neural network-based example retriever informed by the pseudo-labeled data.
SILC achieves state-of-the-art performance on the newly constructed English and Chinese financial entity-level sentiment analysis datasets.
A case study demonstrates that the constructed datasets and SILC method enhance monitoring of the cryptocurrency market.
Speech Foundation Models are used to automate the validation process for crowdsourced speech data.
The cost/quality trade-off in speech data acquisition using automated validation is examined for the first time.
Automated validation with Speech Foundation Models reduces reliance on human validation, achieving substantial cost savings while preserving final data quality.
TASTE fuses transformer-based content embeddings with unsupervised structural embeddings for stance detection.
TASTE integrates content and conversational structure using a Gated Residual Network layer.
Fine-tuning a pre-trained transformer and amalgamating it with social embedding enables TASTE to capture the interplay between content and structure.
TASTE achieves state-of-the-art performance on common stance detection benchmarks.
Comparative evaluations show that jointly leveraging content and structure yields superior stance detection compared to strong baselines.
Logit extrapolation of critical token probabilities beyond the final layer improves contrasting for factuality in decoding.
Layer-wise entropy-guided lower layer selection decouples the selection process from the final layer during decoding.
The combined approach surpasses state-of-the-art factuality performance on multiple datasets.
Analyses reveal that different prompt types respond to different lower-layer selection strategies.
DROWN certifies the robustness of deep neural networks.
DROWN tightens classic LiRPA-based robustness certification methods while preserving similar scalability.
DROWN refines pre-activation bounds of ReLU relaxations using two pairs of linear bounds derived from different relaxations of ReLU units in previous layers.
DROWN achieves higher certified robust accuracy than baseline methods on convolutional neural networks.
DROWN yields larger certified radii than baseline methods on transformer models.
DROWN incurs increased computational runtime compared to baseline methods.
AutoMolCo framework leverages large language models to automatically generate and label predictive molecular concepts.
AutoMolCo enables simple linear models to outperform graph neural networks and large language model in-context learning on several benchmarks.
AutoMolCo operates without human knowledge input while maintaining explainability and allowing easy intervention.
Experiments on MoleculeNet and high-throughput experimentation datasets show that AutoMolCo-induced explainable concept-based models are beneficial for molecular science research.

UNED-ACCESS 2024 is a bilingual dataset of 1003 university entrance exam multiple-choice questions in Spanish and English, manually translated and not publicly released, ensuring minimal contamination for evaluating large language models.
A uniform zero-shot experimental setting is used to evaluate open-source and proprietary models on UNED-ACCESS 2024 and an equivalent subset of MMLU.
Smaller models perform worse than larger models and degrade more rapidly in Spanish than in English.
Model ranking on UNED-ACCESS 2024 is highly correlated with ranking on MMLU, indicating similar contamination effects across models.
Reasoning questions in UNED-ACCESS 2024 are more challenging for models of all sizes compared to other question types.
Explanation regularisation does not substantially increase a classifier’s reliance on plausible tokens when reliance is measured with attribution techniques different from those used for guidance.
Stronger reliance on plausible tokens does not cause out-of-domain performance improvements in text classifiers employing explanation regularisation.
The previously reported connection between explanation regularisation and a classifier’s ability to rely on plausible features is weaker than earlier claims suggested.
Freezing unimportant parameters enables effective differentially private fine-tuning of large-scale pre-trained models.
Gradient-based selection methods identify parameters to fine-tune for differentially private adaptation of pre-trained models.
Experiments on real datasets demonstrate that the proposed freezing and gradient-based selection methods improve utility under differential privacy.
An obfuscation-based data augmentation method reduces bias in toxicity classifiers by adding obfuscated versions of all false positive instances regardless of source community.
The obfuscation-based method maintains false positive rates and F1 scores for both minority and majority communities while reducing bias.
The method is evaluated on toxicity classifiers built with multiple neural models, including multilayer perceptron and masked language models, across several datasets in a multi-community setting.
Four different obfuscators are explored for debiasing within the proposed approach.
Compared to state-of-the-art bias mitigation baselines, the obfuscation-based method reduces bias without incurring performance sacrifices.
DeepSoftDebias uses a neural network to perform soft debiasing of large language model word embeddings.
DeepSoftDebias is evaluated on a variety of state-of-the-art datasets, accuracy metrics, and challenging NLP tasks.
DeepSoftDebias reduces bias across gender, race, and religion more effectively than current state-of-the-art methods.
InCA integrates an external continual learner with in-context learning to enable scalable continual learning without catastrophic forgetting.
The external continual learner is built incrementally to pre-select a small subset of likely classes for each test instance.
Restricting the in-context learning prompt to the selected classes prevents prompt lengths from exceeding token limits while maintaining high performance.
InCA outperforms existing continual learning baselines.
RRHF-V uses rank-responses (one non-hallucination versus multiple hallucinations) to mitigate multimodal hallucinations.
RRHF-V expands the set of hallucinatory responses, enabling the model to learn rich semantic information across multiple image dimensions.
A scene graph-based approach automatically constructs rank-responses in a cost-effective manner.
A novel training objective that combines rank loss and margin loss balances differences between hallucinatory responses within a rank-response, improving image comprehension.
RRHF-V mitigates hallucinations in multimodal large language models and outperforms a pair-response DPO method.
A series of experiments is devised to explain the performance gap of large language models on extractive question answering under domain drift.
Large language models struggle with closed-domain extractive question answering tasks that require retrieving long answer spans.
Certain large language models exhibit weaknesses in discriminating domain-specific word senses, a limitation linked to preprocessing decisions.
Increasing model parameter size does not consistently improve cross-domain generalization for extractive question answering.
Closed-domain extractive question answering datasets differ quantitatively from open-domain datasets, and current large language models have difficulty handling them.
LLM-elicited NLI datasets are created by recreating a portion of the Stanford Natural Language Inference corpus using GPT-4, Llama-2 70b, and Mistral 7b.
Hypothesis-only classifiers are trained to detect annotation artifacts in LLM-generated NLI datasets.
Point-wise mutual information is applied to identify words associated with gender, race, and age terms in LLM-generated NLI datasets.
Fine-tuned BERT hypothesis-only classifiers achieve high accuracy in detecting annotation artifacts in LLM-generated NLI datasets.
Analyses characterize the annotation artifacts and stereotypical biases present in LLM-generated NLI datasets.
Instruction-tuned large language models can be used for zero-shot readability-controlled sentence simplification.
Different types of contextual information influence a model’s ability to generate sentences at a desired readability level in zero-shot readability-controlled sentence simplification.
A trade-off exists between achieving target readability and preserving meaning in zero-shot readability-controlled sentence simplification.
Tested instruction-tuned models struggle to simplify sentences to the lowest readability levels.
Standard automatic evaluation metrics often misinterpret common simplification operations and inaccurately assess readability and meaning preservation in readability-controlled text simplification.
A multi-lingual Part-of-Speech tagger is introduced that attains high accuracy across 1,749 languages.
A realistic evaluation setup is introduced that focuses on typology features likely to be missing.
Using textual data for typology feature prediction provides better-informed predictions than methods relying solely on features from other languages.
The proposed approach outperforms previous work in both the standard and realistic evaluation setups.
URIEL+ enhances linguistic inclusion and usability in a typological and multilingual knowledge base.
URIEL+ expands typological feature coverage for 2898 languages.
URIEL+ provides robust, customizable distance calculations for linguistic vectors.
URIEL+ achieves competitive performance on downstream tasks.
URIEL+ yields distance measures that better align with linguistic distance studies.
Adversarial Word Recovery is introduced as a diagnostic task extending spellchecking to adversarial inputs.
A dataset comprising adversarial examples generated by nine perturbation attack strategies is released, organized by a taxonomy of phonetic, typo, and visual attacks.
Proprietary large language models achieve word recovery performance comparable to or exceeding human performance on the Adversarial Word Recovery task.
Open-source large language models exhibit a substantial performance gap relative to humans on visual attacks in the Adversarial Word Recovery task.

Word recovery performance without context correlates with word recovery performance with context for open-source models, influencing downstream hateful, offensive, and toxic classification performance.
A small Byt5 model fine‑tuned to recover visually attacked words improves robustness of language models against such attacks.
A feasibility analysis of large language models for relation extraction is performed.
Five state-of-the-art large language models are evaluated across more than 2100 experiments to assess robustness to complex data characteristics in relation extraction.
Large language models are found to lack sufficient robustness for handling complex data characteristics in relation extraction.
State-of-the-art large language models evaluated with three learning frameworks across six healthcare tasks reveal significant performance challenges in real-world healthcare applications.
Large language models exhibit persistent fairness issues across demographic groups in healthcare task predictions.
Providing explicit demographic information to large language models yields mixed effects on performance and fairness.
Large language models can infer demographic details without explicit input, raising concerns about biased health predictions.
Using large language models as autonomous agents with access to up-to-date guidelines does not guarantee performance improvement on healthcare tasks.
CoMIF is a unified response generation framework that simultaneously models complex multiple interaction factors for conversation generation.
CoMIF includes a dynamic perception module that constructs a directed collaborative graph to jointly learn temporal dynamics of each factor and cross-dependencies among factors.
CoMIF includes a scalable post-adaptation module that introduces token-level factor signals to generate responses with multiple factors.
Experiments across multiple datasets show that CoMIF achieves superior performance in generating more human-like responses with appropriate multiple factors compared to state-of-the-art methods.
Cross-domain evaluation of text classifiers for classroom discourse codes is performed on two English-language classroom datasets from literacy and math domains.
Models trained and tested on the same or similar educational domains achieve high accuracy and generalizability.
Few-shot and zero-shot classification approaches demonstrate greater resilience than supervised models when training data is limited in new domains.
Incorporating dialog-level context with each talk turn improves the accuracy of generative models for discourse classification.
A novel interpretation of cosine similarity as the sum of semantic similarities over ICA axes is proposed.
Normalized ICA-transformed embeddings exhibit sparsity that enhances interpretability of each axis.
Semantic similarity defined by the product of ICA components represents the shared meaning between two embeddings along each axis.
A method for selecting statistically significant ICA axes is proposed based on derived probability distributions of components and their products.
The proposed interpretation and normalized ICA-transformed embeddings are demonstrated to be effective through numerical examples and experiments.
Explanation-consistency finetuning adapts large language models to generate more consistent natural-language explanations on related examples.
Explanation-consistency finetuning finetunes large language models on synthetic data that contain consistent explanations.
Explanation-consistency finetuning improves explanation consistency on multiple question-answering datasets and generalizes to out-of-distribution datasets.
A data augmentation approach using a Variational Autoencoder captures relation-wise distributions of entity pair representations for document-level relation extraction.
The approach augments data for underrepresented relations in document-level relation extraction.
The VAE's latent space is parameterized with a diffusion model to better capture the multi-label nature of document-level relation extraction.
A hierarchical training framework integrates the VAE-based augmentation module into document-level relation extraction systems.
The method outperforms state-of-the-art models on benchmark document-level relation extraction datasets, effectively addressing the long-tail distribution problem.
A novel Contrastive In-Context Learning RAG architecture is introduced.
Advanced RAG system designs integrate query expansion.
Advanced RAG system designs incorporate various novel retrieval strategies.

A systematic analysis evaluates the impact of language model size, prompt design, document chunk size, knowledge base size, retrieval stride, query expansion techniques, Contrastive In-Context Learning knowledge bases, multilingual knowledge bases, and Focus Mode on response quality in RAG systems.
Actionable insights are provided for balancing contextual richness and retrieval-generation efficiency in RAG frameworks.
A fine-grained code search model is introduced that consists of a cross-modal encoder, a mapping layer, and a classification layer.
The cross-modal encoder employs the pre-trained GraphCodeBERT model to align code and query features.
The mapping layer uses a co-attention network to capture fine-grained interactions between code and query, enabling precise identification of key code segments relevant to the query.
The classification layer incorporates instruction learning techniques that leverage contextual reasoning to improve query-code matching accuracy.
The proposed model outperforms existing code search methods across multiple programming language datasets.
Courtroom-LLM is a multi-LLM framework that simulates courtroom roles such as prosecutors, defense attorneys, and judges for ambiguous text classification.
The structured multi-LLM setup improves decision-making accuracy in ambiguous text classification scenarios.
Courtroom-LLM outperforms traditional single-LLM classifiers on various text classification tasks.
Courtroom-LLM outperforms simpler multi-LLM configurations on various text classification tasks.
RoleBreak provides a systematic analysis of character hallucination as an attack in role-playing language model systems.
RoleBreak identifies query sparsity and role-query conflict as core mechanisms driving character hallucination.
RoleBreakEval is a dataset for evaluating hallucination mitigation techniques in role-playing language model systems.
Narrator Mode generates supplemental narration context to mitigate role-query conflicts and improve query generalization in role-playing language models.
Narrator Mode reduces character hallucinations and improves fidelity to character roles, queries, and overall narrative coherence compared to traditional refusal-based strategies.
EffiQA is a collaborative framework that enables efficient question answering over knowledge graphs through an iterative paradigm.
EffiQA performs global planning by leveraging the commonsense capabilities of large language models to explore potential reasoning pathways.
EffiQA offloads semantic pruning to a small plug-in model to achieve efficient knowledge graph exploration.
EffiQA employs self-reflection on exploration results to improve global planning and knowledge graph exploration.
Empirical evaluation on multiple knowledge base question answering benchmarks shows that EffiQA attains an optimal balance between reasoning accuracy and computational cost.
Token Reduction using CLIP Metric (TRIM) reduces image tokens in multimodal large language models.
TRIM improves computational efficiency of multimodal large language models while maintaining performance.
TRIM is inspired by human attention patterns in visual question answering tasks.
TRIM has been evaluated on multiple datasets, demonstrating significant reduction in computational overhead without performance loss.
S+NER is a framework that leverages sentence-level information for out-of-entity named entity recognition.
S+NER exploits a pre-trained language model's understanding of target entity sentence-level context using a template set.
S+NER refines sentence-level representations with positive and negative templates through contrastive learning and a template pooling method.
S+NER achieves superior out-of-entity named entity recognition performance compared to prior state-of-the-art models.
Re-Cent extracts relation triplets in a zero-shot setting using only unseen relation labels in a unified model.
Re-Cent employs two span-based extractors to identify subject and object spans for each relation label, forming span-pairs.
Re-Cent includes a relation-based correction mechanism that refines extracted triplets by computing relevance between span-pairs and relation labels.
Re-Cent achieves state-of-the-art performance on zero-shot relation triplet extraction while using fewer parameters.
Re-Cent does not require synthetic data or manual labor for training.
LLMERE transforms event relation extraction into a question-and-answer task that can produce multiple answers.
LLMERE extracts all events related to a specified event simultaneously, reducing time complexity from quadratic to linear.
LLMERE employs a partitioning strategy that processes subsets of events to enhance coverage of extraction results.
LLMERE generates rationales for extracted relations, providing event coreference information or transitive chains of event relations.
LLMERE achieves significant improvements over baseline methods on three widely used event relation extraction datasets.
DORA generates task-adaptive and diverse reflection advice for LLM-based agents.
DORA employs an external small language model to dynamically generate prompts for the reflection LLM.
DORA optimizes prompt generation using a non-gradient Bayesian Optimization algorithm based on agent feedback.
DORA mitigates the Early Stop Reflection phenomenon, allowing agents to maintain iterative improvements in long-term complex tasks.
Experiments in MiniWoB++ and Alfworld demonstrate that DORA enables performance boosts for agents in long-term complex tasks.
MCA is a model-based reinforcement learning framework that automatically constructs causal chains for dialogue policy learning.
